html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirty-Seven -- Complete

Words of the Luminaries

DIBREI HA-ME'OROT -- 4QDibHam

A Weekly Prayer Cycle -- DSS Attested

Four Prayers Preserved

Remember O Creator the Multitude of Your Mercies -- You Chose Avraham and Made Covenant With Him -- You Created Man in Your Image -- You Blessed the Seventh Day and Made It Holy -- We Are the Work of Your Hands

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 4 Qumran -- 4Q504-506 -- Hebrew Primary

Dated Approximately 150 BCE -- Among the Oldest Qumran Liturgical Texts

Weekly Prayer Cycle -- Sunday Through Shabbat

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

WORDS OF THE LUMINARIES -- THE WEEKLY PRAYER CYCLE

WORDS OF THE LUMINARIES — SUNDAY PRAYER A Prayer for the First Day — Remember O Creator the Multitude of Your Mercies — You Are Long-Suffering and Full of Compassion — We Have Sinned Against You — Do Not Cast Us Away From Your Presence

1 Remember O Creator the multitude of your mercies and your compassion from of old, for you are long-suffering and full of grace and truth and abundantly forgiving of sin and transgression.

2 We have sinned against you from our youth until this day and your hand has not turned away from us.

3 For your great name's sake do not turn away from us and do not cast us away from your presence.

4 You are a Creator of truth and all your ways are righteousness — your face shines upon your people who are in covenant with you.

5 And you have not hidden your face from your servant for all the evil that we have done before you.

6 For you are a Creator of compassion and grace — you are slow to anger and full of steadfast love and faithfulness.

7 For you have remembered your covenant with our fathers and your steadfast love with the generations.

8 And you did not abandon us in the land of our enemies but rather redeemed us from the hand of the oppressor.

9 And now on this first day we pray before you for your mercies upon your people.

10 Turn your face toward us and let your glorious presence be known among us.

11 And let your holy name be sanctified in all the congregation of those who call upon your name.

12 Blessed are you O Creator who bestows covenant love upon your people Yisra'EL forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Words of the Luminaries (4QDibHam) was found in Cave 4 at Qumran in multiple copies — 4Q504, 4Q505, and 4Q506. The title Dibrei HaMe'orot — Words of the Luminaries — appears in the text itself. The document is a weekly prayer cycle of seven prayers — one for each day — structured around a historical survey of Creator's covenant faithfulness from creation through the Exodus and into the exile and restoration. 4Q504 is the most complete copy and dates to approximately 150 BCE — making it one of the oldest liturgical compositions at Qumran. Sunday's prayer focuses on confession and appeal to covenant mercy.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves extensive confessional prayer material in Daniyel 9 and Nekhemyah 9 that shares the same theological structure as the Words of the Luminaries: historical recital of covenant faithfulness followed by confession of sin followed by appeal to covenant mercy. The Words of the Luminaries is the Qumran community's weekly liturgical expression of this covenant theological structure — the same confession made daily and weekly that Daniyel and Nekhemyah made at pivotal historical moments.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Tehillim preserve numerous parallel confessional prayers — particularly Tehillim 78, 105, and 106 — which combine historical recital and covenant confession. The Words of the Luminaries belongs to this same covenant liturgical tradition. The weekly structure — seven prayers for seven days — reflects the covenant sanctification of time established in Bereshit 1: each day of the week is marked by a specific covenant orientation in prayer.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Words of the Luminaries is the only Qumran document that provides a complete structured weekly prayer liturgy. It establishes that the Qumran covenant community maintained a formal daily and weekly prayer cycle — a structured liturgical life that stands alongside the legal and eschatological texts as the covenant community's actual devotional practice. The document's historical surveys — tracing Creator's covenant faithfulness from creation through the patriarchs through the Exodus — place the community's present prayer within the grand covenant narrative.

WORDS OF THE LUMINARIES — MONDAY PRAYER A Prayer for the Second Day — You Chose Avraham and Made Covenant With Him — You Led His Seed Out of Egypt — You Gave Them a Torah of Truth — Remember Your Servants the Patriarchs

1 Remember O Creator the deeds of your love from the days of old — your covenant with Avraham your friend, with Yitzhak your servant, and with Ya'akov whom you chose from among all nations.

2 You brought Avraham out of Ur of the Chaldeans and changed his name because you loved him.

3 And you swore to him by your own self that you would multiply his seed like the stars of heaven and like the sand of the sea.

4 And you kept your promise and raised up from him a great people and took them out of the land of Egypt with a mighty hand and a stretched out arm.

5 And you split the sea before them and led them through the wilderness and gave them water from the rock.

6 And you gave them a Torah of truth and statutes of life and a covenant of peace.

7 But they did not keep your covenant and they rejected your Torah and did not walk in your commandments.

8 And you gave them into the hand of their enemies and they were afflicted in the lands of their captivity.

9 But even in their afflictions you did not cast them away or destroy them or break your covenant with them.

10 For you are a Creator of compassion and you remembered your covenant from the days of old.

11 And now on this second day we pray before you — remember your servants Avraham, Yitzhak, and Ya'akov.

12 Turn from your fierce anger and show mercy to your people and forgive all our transgressions for your name's sake.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q504 preserves substantial portions of the Monday Prayer. The historical recital of Creator's covenant faithfulness in verses 1-9 follows the pattern of the great confessional prayers of the covenant canon — Nekhemyah 9, Tehillim 106, and Daniyel 9. The Monday Prayer traces the covenant from Avraham through the Exodus and into the exile, establishing that the community's present prayer stands within an unbroken covenant history. Creator's faithfulness throughout the historical failures of the covenant people is the theological basis for the present appeal to covenant mercy.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves the covenant with Avraham in Bereshit 15 and 17 — the same covenant cited in verse 1-3 of the Monday Prayer. The weekly rehearsal of the Abrahamic covenant in the Words of the Luminaries reflects the Qumran community's conviction that every prayer offered in the present age is offered within the framework of the original covenant promise. The covenant with Avraham is not a historical memory — it is the living foundation of every covenant prayer.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Nekhemyah 9 provides the closest structural parallel to the Monday Prayer's historical recital — you alone are YHWH, you made the heaven and the heaven of heavens, and all their host, the earth and all that is on it — and traces the covenant faithfulness from creation through Avraham through the Exodus. The Words of the Luminaries is the Qumran community's weekly liturgical equivalent of the great confession of Nekhemyah 9.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Monday Prayer's structure — covenant faithfulness of Creator from Avraham through the Exodus, followed by covenant failure of the people, followed by Creator's refusal to break the covenant even in judgment — is the standard covenant theological framework that runs through the entire Qumran corpus. The Damascus Document's Admonition follows precisely this structure. The Words of the Luminaries institutionalises it as weekly liturgy: every Monday the community rehearses the same covenant theology that grounds their entire existence as a covenant community.

WORDS OF THE LUMINARIES — FRIDAY PRAYER A Prayer for the Sixth Day — Creator Made the Heavens and the Earth — He Created Man in His Image — He Breathed Into Him the Breath of Life — We Are the Sons of Your Covenant — Do Not Withhold Your Mercies

1 Blessed are you O Creator of all things who created the heavens and the earth and all their host.

2 And you stretched out the heavens like a curtain and established the earth upon the waters.

3 And you created man from the dust of the earth and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life.

4 And you set him as ruler over all the earth and over all the beasts of the field and the birds of the air and the fish of the sea.

5 And you made him in your image and after your likeness to bear your glory in all the earth.

6 But he transgressed your covenant and walked in the stubbornness of his heart.

7 And yet you did not cast him away entirely but gave him sons and daughters and you kept watch over him.

8 For we are the sons of your covenant, the children of Avraham your friend to whom you swore an oath.

9 And we are the people of your possession whom you chose from among all the peoples of the earth.

10 And your name has been called over us forever and we shall not be cut off for your name's sake.

11 Do not withhold your mercies from us and do not hide your face from your servants.

12 For you are our Creator and we are your people — the work of your hands and the inheritance of your covenant forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q504 preserves the Friday Prayer, which is the creation prayer of the weekly cycle. Its placement on the sixth day — the day on which Creator made humanity — makes it the theological foundation of the entire prayer cycle: before the Shabbat rest comes the creation prayer acknowledging humanity's original covenant relationship with Creator. The Friday Prayer's account of creation draws on Bereshit 1-2 and grounds the covenant community's identity in the original creation intention — humanity made in Creator's image to bear his glory in the earth.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition preserves the creation narrative of Bereshit 1-2 in full, including the creation of humanity in Creator's image and the breathing of life into the nostrils. The Friday Prayer applies the creation theology directly to the covenant community: we are the sons of your covenant — the community understands itself as the living continuation of the original creation purpose. The restoration of the Adamic glory — which both the Damascus Document and the Community Rule promise to the faithful — begins with the recognition in the Friday Prayer that Creator made humanity to bear his glory.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bereshit 1:26-27 — let us make man in our image and after our likeness — and 2:7 — he breathed into his nostrils the breath of life — are the foundational covenant texts for verses 3-5 of the Friday Prayer. The Words of the Luminaries places the creation of humanity at the centre of the sixth-day prayer because the Shabbat that follows is the covenant sign of the completed creation — Bereshit 2:1-3. The Friday Prayer and the Shabbat Prayer together express the complete covenant theology of creation and covenant rest.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — for you are our Creator and we are your people — the work of your hands and the inheritance of your covenant forever. The covenant formula — you are our Creator, we are your people — is the most concentrated expression of the covenant relationship in the covenant canon. It appears in this form in Vayikra 26:12, Yirmeyahu 7:23, and Yehezkel 36:28. The Words of the Luminaries uses it as the closing statement of the Friday Prayer — the day of creation — to affirm that the covenant community is the living embodiment of Creator's creation purpose: humanity made to be his people in his earth.

WORDS OF THE LUMINARIES — SHABBAT PRAYER A Prayer for the Seventh Day — Creator Rested on the Seventh Day — He Blessed It and Made It Holy — Remember the Shabbat to Keep It Holy — A Covenant Sign Between Creator and Yisra'EL Forever

1 Blessed are you O Creator who blessed the seventh day and sanctified it from all the days of the week.

2 For on it you rested from all your work and you called it holy and you made it a sign of the covenant between you and your people Yisra'EL forever.

3 You completed all your work in six days and on the seventh day you ceased and were refreshed.

4 And you commanded us through your servant Mosheh to keep the Shabbat day and to sanctify it.

5 To remember that you brought us out of the land of Egypt with a mighty hand and a stretched out arm.

6 And to remember that you rested from your work on the seventh day and blessed it and made it holy.

7 Let your presence rest among your people on this holy day and may your glory fill the sanctuary.

8 May the angels of your holiness sing praises before you and may all the holy ones rejoice in your presence.

9 And may the sons of your covenant walk in your light and may we serve you with a whole heart on this holy day.

10 Do not let any transgression or uncleanness defile this day among your people.

11 For the Shabbat is a delight before you and a covenant sign between you and your people throughout their generations.

12 Blessed are you O Creator who sanctifies the Shabbat and gives it as a gift of rest to your people Yisra'EL forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q504 preserves portions of the Shabbat Prayer, the culmination of the seven-day liturgical cycle. The Shabbat Prayer draws together the two foundational covenant rationales for Shabbat observance — the creation rest of Bereshit 2:2-3 and the redemption from Egypt of Devarim 5:15 — and frames the entire weekly prayer cycle. The Qumran community's meticulous Shabbat observance — documented in the Damascus Document's extensive Shabbat laws — is here expressed in its devotional form: the Shabbat is not merely a legal requirement but a covenant gift and a sign of the living covenant relationship between Creator and his people.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition preserves both the creation Shabbat of Bereshit 2 and the Sinai Shabbat commandment of Shemot 20 and Devarim 5. The Ethiopian covenant community's strong Shabbat observance — maintained through the Beta Yisra'EL tradition — reflects the same covenant conviction the Shabbat Prayer expresses: the Shabbat is a permanent covenant sign, not a ceremonial addition. The Words of the Luminaries and the Ethiopian Shabbat theology stand together as witnesses to the original covenant sanctity of the seventh day.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Shemot 31:16-17 — the children of Yisra'EL shall keep the Shabbat, observing the Shabbat throughout their generations as a perpetual covenant — provides the direct covenant foundation for verse 11 of the Shabbat Prayer. The covenant sign language — alot — between Creator and his people is the most explicit covenant designation in the entire Torah, and the Words of the Luminaries places it at the climax of the weekly liturgical cycle.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Shabbat Prayer's description of the angels singing praises before Creator on the Shabbat — verse 8 — connects the Words of the Luminaries directly to the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice, which describes the angelic worship of the heavenly sanctuary in elaborate detail. The Qumran community understood the earthly Shabbat prayer as participation in the heavenly Shabbat worship — when the covenant community prays on the Shabbat they join their voices with the angels of holiness who sing before Creator in the heavenly sanctuary. This participatory theology of worship is one of the most distinctive features of the Qumran covenant liturgical tradition.

WORDS OF THE LUMINARIES -- COMPLETE

Volume Thirty-Seven -- The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

FOR YOU ARE OUR CREATOR AND WE ARE YOUR PEOPLE

THE WORK OF YOUR HANDS AND THE INHERITANCE OF YOUR COVENANT FOREVER

Words of the Luminaries -- Friday Prayer -- Dead Sea Scrolls 4Q504
